burial.inscription: Here lies Inter'd ye Remains of Mrs. Susanna Rawson Relict of ye late Rev'd Mr. Grindall Rawson, Pastor of a Church of Christ in Mendon & Daughter of ye Rev'd Mr. John Wilson 1st Minister of ye Gospel in Medfield who changed mortality for life Aug. 23, 1691. She was born Dec. 1, 1664, & died July 8, 1748, in ye 84th year of her age.

sources.full: Gary Boyd Roberts, Ancestors of American Presidents
sources.label: Roberts 2009

narrative_blocks.header: Wrote Cotton Mather a eulogy for her husband
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Grindal Rawson died on February 6, 1715, Susanna wrote a long letter to Cotton Mather — a detailed portrait of her husband's forty-year ministry at Mendon, his Indian mission work, his character, and his final hours. His last words were "Come Lord Jesus, come quickly!" The letter was published in the Rawson Family Memorial. Cotton Mather quoted it in the sermon he preached at Rawson's funeral.

narrative_blocks.header: Her gravestone names her father, not husband
narrative_blocks.body: Her gravestone in Mendon reads: "Here lies inter'd ye Remains of Mrs. Susanna Rawson, Relict of ye late Rev. Mr. Grindal Rawson... and daughter to ye Rev. Mr. John Wilson 1st Minister of ye Gospel in Medfield." She outlived her husband by 33 years, dying July 8, 1748, age 83.

narrative_blocks.header: Ancestor of President Taft through both Taft brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Her granddaughter Mary Dorr Taft married Moses Taft, and her great-granddaughter Rhoda Rawson Taft married Aaron Taft — Moses's and Aaron's fathers were brothers. President William Howard Taft descends through the Aaron Taft line.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law, the Bay Colony’s secretary

She was born Dec. 1, 1664, & died July 8, 1748, in ye 84th year of her age.

Mr. John Wilson 1st Minister of ye Gospel in Medfield." She outlived her husband by 33 years, dying July 8, 1748, age 83.

cross_connections.display_label: was her father-in-law, the Bay Colony’s secretary

notable.notable_blurb: Mendon minister; Algonquian translator; Cotton Mather classmate

quotes.text: To my Beloved Wife Mrs Susanna Rawson who tho last in mention yet deserveth the first in my best and sincere affections
quotes.attribution: Rev. Grindal Rawson, Will dated Jan 24, 1714

narrative_blocks.header: Named for Elizabeth I's Archbishop of Canterbury
narrative_blocks.body: He was named "Grindal" after Edmund Grindal, Archbishop of Canterbury under Elizabeth I — his great-great-grandmother's brother, per the Harvard commencement of 1678. At that ceremony President Urian Oakes called him "sprung from a most illustrious stock" and prayed that Rawson might "in learning, holiness and excellence of character, resemble both Wilson and Grindal."

narrative_blocks.header: Mastered Algonquian; preached and translated for natives
narrative_blocks.body: He learned the Algonquian language and preached sermons in it to local Nipmuc parishioners — translating Thomas Shepard's Sincere Convert, John Cotton's Spiritual Milk, and a Confession of Faith. Cotton Mather called him 'a Master that had scarce an Equal' in the language.

narrative_blocks.header: Ordered to sail with the Canada invasion
narrative_blocks.body: In July 1692 the General Court ordered four ministers, Rawson among them, to accompany the forces in the expedition against Canada, “to carry on the worshipping of God.” Whether he sailed with Sir William Phipps is unknown.

cross_connections.display_label: carried the Mendon pastor’s name into the Talcott line

bio.title: Reverend
bio.photo_notes: Gravestone portrait, Mendon burying ground — primary for authenticity.

will.notes: Cordwainer of Bristol, "far advanced in years." Named sons Benjamin, John, and Grindall Reynolds; daughters Mary Rawson, Sarah Weld, and Ann Reynolds.

open_questions: Given name of husband Morse unknown — no further record found in EH or secondary sources

narrative_blocks.header: Second Mary — first Mary the twin died young
narrative_blocks.body: Mary Reynolds Rawson was born November 20, 1716 in Bristol Rhode Island — the second daughter named Mary, after the first Mary (twin to her sister Anne) had died young earlier in November 1716. The replacement naming worked: this second Mary lived to nearly eighty-seven, dying in 1803 after surviving two marriages and bearing children in both. Her first marriage to Dr. Samuel Morse left her widowed with three young children when he died in 1743.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her first cousin Edward Rawson in 1748
narrative_blocks.body: After widowhood in 1743, Mary remarried Deacon Edward Rawson — her first cousin through their mothers Susannah Rawson Reynolds and Wilson Rawson who were siblings, both fifth-generation Hooker descendants via the Sarah Hooker line. Their marriage was a documented Pierpont-Reynolds first-cousin union. The 'Aunt Reynolds' relationship was sometimes recorded ambiguously as grandmother rather than mother, but Mary was the daughter, not the granddaughter, of Susannah Rawson Reynolds.

narrative_blocks.header: His two sons each married an Allen sister
narrative_blocks.body: Both his sons Edmund and Abner married daughters of the same Allen family of Medway — Martha Allen and Mary Allen respectively. He was Deacon Edmund Rawson of Uxbridge; his sons' double Allen marriage kept the two families closely bound across a generation.

bio.notes: Also given as "Howard" in EH — Hayward confirmed via Bridgewater vital records

narrative_blocks.header: Two Allen sisters married two Rawson brothers
narrative_blocks.body: Martha Allen married Edmund Rawson and her sister Mary Allen married Abner Rawson — both Edmund and Abner being sons of Dea. Edmund Rawson of Uxbridge. Two Allen sisters from Medway, two Rawson brothers, one generation.

sources.notes: Children: Silas, Timothy, Rhoda b. Oct 4, 1749 m. Aaron Taft; marriage: "Abner and Mary Allen of Medway, in Medway, May 17, 1745"

narrative_blocks.header: Two Allen sisters of Medway married two Rawson
narrative_blocks.body: Martha Allen married Edmund Rawson and her sister Mary Allen married Abner Rawson — both Edmund and Abner being sons of Dea. Edmund Rawson of Uxbridge. Two Allen sisters from Medway, two Rawson brothers, one generation.

sources.short: Vital Records of Uxbridge MA / Rawson source

parents.mother_research_notes: Priscilla Gardner of Nantucket — Gardners are a prominent Nantucket family

cross_connections.display_label: Her husband's great-grandfather bought Nantucket in 1659

narrative_blocks.header: Married into one of Nantucket's founding families
narrative_blocks.body: She married into the Coffin family of Nantucket, Massachusetts — descendants of Tristram Coffin, who in 1659 bought Nantucket Island for thirty pounds and two beaver hats. The Coffins were among the island's most prominent founders.

narrative_blocks.header: Son Joshua continued the line through cousin marriage
narrative_blocks.body: Her son Joshua Coffin (born October 10, 1737) married his cousin Catharine Coffin, daughter of Nathaniel Coffin and Mary Sheffield. Cousin marriage was common among the Nantucket Coffins navigating the small island's tight kinship network.

bio.notes: Named for grandfather Rev. Grindal Rawson (I00005)

political_offices.title: Member, Massachusetts Legislature
political_offices.notes: Frequently a member of various committees and the Legislature before and during the Revolutionary War

political_offices.title: Member, Constitutional Convention
political_offices.notes: Member of Convention to form a Constitution for Massachusetts, 1779

cross_connections.display_label: His aunt — her granddaughter Mary Morse became his wife
His first cousin — her daughter Mary Morse became his wife
His aunt's husband — together they raised the Morse line

military_service.notes: "Staunch opposer of British rule" — leading figure in Mendon committees during the war

narrative_blocks.header: Named his firstborn son 'Hooker' in 1749
narrative_blocks.body: In 1749, four generations after Thomas Hooker's death, he named his firstborn son Hooker Rawson. The deliberate act of family memory was carried in a generation that knew their Hooker descent only as received tradition.

narrative_blocks.header: Married his first cousin once removed
narrative_blocks.body: He married Mary Morse — granddaughter of his aunt Susannah Rawson Reynolds of Bristol, Rhode Island. Edward Hooker confirmed the relationship in his 1909 genealogy as 'Gr. dau. of his Aunt Reynolds.'

narrative_blocks.header: Revolutionary War deacon and town leader
narrative_blocks.body: He served in the Revolution and lived to age 86 in Leicester, Massachusetts. EB Crane wrote that 'Nature did much for him' — he was a leading man in the town's affairs.

death.cause: EH: "killed in the Indian Wars." No date or location given.

military_service.war: Indian Wars (French and Indian War era)

sources.notes: Confirmed: Rhoda d. of Abner and Mary, Oct. 4, 1749 is the Taft wife

open_questions.question: Is Josiah Rawson (b.~1730s, m. Hannah Bass, Grafton/Warwick MA) a sibling or cousin of this entry?
open_questions.notes: The Rawson genealogy book (entry #25) documents Josiah Rawson marrying Hannah Bass of Braintree, residing in Grafton then Warwick MA. His son Lemuel (#163-4 in Rawson book) settled in Richfield, Summit County OH. Lemuel's son Dr. Secretary Rawson (b.1796) was Richfield's first physician (1824+), practiced 40 years, founded the town's first library (1825), and was active in temperance work. If Josiah is a Hooker-Rawson descendant (gen 4-5), then Dr. Secretary Rawson is a gen 6-7 Hooker descendant who settled on the same Western Reserve land as John Newton (HD2925) and Eunice Newton Oviatt (HD2934) — all Connecticut Hooker descendants converging in Richfield OH. Action: Match Josiah Rawson to Rawson genealogy entry chain in the project.

bio.notes: Second child named "Hooker Rawson" in the Rawson family — this one in the John Rawson household; died age 2 in 1741. Compare HD0282 Hooker Rawson (son of Edward Rawson, b.1749, survived). The name was carried across multiple Rawson branches.

narrative_blocks.header: She shared his birthday, died on her anniversary
narrative_blocks.body: Born June 22 like her husband, nine years apart, Mary Rawson Dorr died on April 9, 1776 — fifty-two years to the day after her wedding. Her stone reads “Dust thou art and unto dust shalt thou return.”

church_roles.notes: Successor to Rev. Grindal Rawson (I00005), his future father-in-law. Settled 1715. "Highly respected and very much beloved and esteemed by his people."

cross_connections.display_label: Succeeded Rev. Grindal Rawson as pastor of Mendon — his.

notable.notable_blurb: Pastor of Mendon for fifty-two years

bio.title: Reverend
bio.bio_blurb: Pastor of Mendon for fifty-two years

narrative_blocks.header: Fifty-two years in his father-in-law’s pulpit
narrative_blocks.body: Settled at Mendon in 1715 as successor to Rev. Grindal Rawson, Dorr then married Rawson’s daughter — keeping the pulpit in one extended family for the better part of a century. He died “in his 79th year and the 52nd of his ministry”; his epitaph reads, “His temper was mild and placid.”

narrative_blocks.header: He succeeded the father, married the daughter
narrative_blocks.body: Ordained at Mendon in 1716 to succeed the late Grindal Rawson, Dorr married Rawson’s daughter Mary eight years later — and held the pulpit fifty-two years, until his death. The son of Edward and Elizabeth Hawley Dorr of Roxbury, he was Harvard, class of 1711.

narrative_blocks.header: The Association met at his house
narrative_blocks.body: First Moderator of the Mendon Association from its 1751 founding — which gathered at his home for its first five years — he remained its standing Moderator until death, favored Jonathan Edwards’s views, and signed the ministers’ testimonial for the revival of 1740.

cross_connections.display_label: Both Rawson cousins married into the Taft family.

burial.plot_notes: New Town Cemetery, Arrowsic, Maine. FindAGrave #78061772

cross_connections.display_label: grew up in Grindall’s Hadlyme congregation, later its deacon

narrative_blocks.header: South Hadley’s first settled minister
narrative_blocks.body: Grindall followed his father into the pulpit — Harvard 1728, then nine years as South Hadley’s first settled minister before his 1745 introduction to the Hadlyme parish, partly in Lyme and partly in East Haddam, where he remained for life.

narrative_blocks.header: A lifetime in his father’s Hadlyme house
narrative_blocks.body: Edmund studied divinity and preached occasionally but was never settled, spending his long life in the East Haddam house his father had occupied. He outlived seven of his twelve children.

parents.father_research_notes: Son of Rev. John Wilson Sr., first minister First Church Boston

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Medfield church — served forty years
narrative_blocks.body: He became the first minister of Medfield, Massachusetts in 1651 and served the congregation for forty years until his death in 1691. He had previously served as a colleague of Rev. Richard Mather at Dorchester, and received his education at Harvard College in 1642.

narrative_blocks.header: Harvard 1642 — the first graduating class
narrative_blocks.body: He attended Harvard College in 1642, effectively the first graduating class of the institution. His father Rev. John Wilson Sr. was a close ally of Rev. Thomas Hooker, and John Jr. married Hooker's daughter Sarah — bringing together two of the founding families of Puritan New England.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of Boston's founding minister
narrative_blocks.body: His father, Rev. John Wilson Sr., was the first minister of the First Church of Boston from its founding in 1630 until his death in 1667 — the man who formally excommunicated Anne Hutchinson in 1638 and who supervised the execution of Mary Dyer in 1660.

narrative_blocks.header: Fell four stories — taken up for dead
narrative_blocks.body: Cotton Mather records that as a child, Wilson "fell upon his head from a loft four stories high into the street; from whence he was taken up for dead, and so battered and bruised and bloody with his fall, that it struck horror into the beholders." His father's prayers were credited with his recovery. He went on to serve Medfield forty years as minister, physician, and schoolmaster.

career.notes: Studied at Eaton's school (introductory to Harvard). Testified to ill-treatment of scholars by Eaton.
Colleague of Rev. Richard Mather at Dorchester. Known as "the gracious and godly Mr. Wilson."
Settled 1651. Ordained pastor Oct 12, 1652. Also served as physician and schoolmaster. Served 40 years until his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Lived to age 95
narrative_blocks.body: Lived to age 95

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived husband by 34 years
narrative_blocks.body: Outlived husband by 34 years

narrative_blocks.header: Her son married her sister Mary's daughter
narrative_blocks.body: Her son John Wilson married Sarah Newton — the daughter of Mary Hooker Newton, Sarah's own sister. Both of Thomas Hooker's daughters had children of the same generation who married each other, making their grandchildren the first documented double-descent in the Hooker tree.

cross_connections.display_label: Her son John Wilson married his first cousin Sarah Newton.
was her father-in-law and Boston’s first minister

parents.father_research_notes: Chaplain to Archbishop of Canterbury Edmund Grindal; prebend of St Paul's, London.

institutions.institution_blurb: First minister of First Church Boston from its founding in 1630 until his death in 1667.

notable.notable_blurb: First minister of Boston; excommunicated Anne Hutchinson

cross_connections.display_label: Both prosecuted Hutchinson — Shepard's voice, Wilson's verdict.
was Hooker’s daughter and married his son John

narrative_blocks.header: He excommunicated Anne Hutchinson from the pulpit
narrative_blocks.body: At the conclusion of her church trial in March 1638, Wilson delivered the final blow: "Forasmuch as you, Mrs. Hutchinson, have highly transgressed and offended...I doe cast you out and deliver you up to Sathan...I command you in the name of Christ Jesus...as a Leper to withdraw your selfe out of the Congregation." Hutchinson left the colony within a week.

narrative_blocks.header: Mary Dyer: nay, I repent not
narrative_blocks.body: On June 1, 1660, Wilson watched his former parishioner Mary Dyer approach the Boston gallows for the second time after returning to the colony against orders. He called to her: "Mary Dyer, O repent, O repent, and be not so deluded and carried away by deceit of the devil." She replied: "Nay, man, I am not now to repent." The ladder was kicked away.

narrative_blocks.header: His son married Thomas Hooker's daughter
narrative_blocks.body: His son John Wilson Jr. attended Harvard in 1642 and married Sarah Hooker, daughter of Rev. Thomas Hooker, founder of Hartford. The two men likely formed a friendship at Emmanuel College, Cambridge, years before either came to New England.

narrative_blocks.header: Called himself vile — refused any portrait
narrative_blocks.body: When urged to sit for a portrait, Wilson replied: "What! Such a poor vile creature as I am! Shall my picture be drawn?

quotes.text: I doe cast you out and...deliver you up to Sathan...I command you in the name of Ch[rist] Je[sus] and of this Church as a Leper to withdraw your selfe out of the Congregation.
quotes.attribution: Rev. John Wilson Sr., excommunicating Anne Hutchinson, March 1638

career.notes: Chaplain to the Countess of Leicester and other prominent families.
Suspended and reinstated several times for Puritan nonconformity.
First minister of the settlers at Charlestown, then Boston. Installed teacher Aug 27, 1630. Served until his death.

education.notes: Four years; chosen to speak Latin oration before duc de Biron, ambassador from Henry IV of France.
M.A. 1613. Noted for Puritan advocacy. Likely formed friendship with Thomas Hooker here.

bio.title: Reverend
bio.photo_notes: Line drawing, Massachusetts Historical Society; said to have been made after his death as Wilson refused to sit for a portrait.

narrative_blocks.header: She refused to cross to New England
narrative_blocks.body: Elizabeth Mansfield long resisted following her husband to Massachusetts; Margaret Winthrop’s 1631 letters marveled that she was “more averse than ever she was” before she finally sailed in 1632.

burial.plot_notes: Exact location unknown; earliest surviving identifiable gravestone in cemetery dates to 1648 Memorial plaque text: "Thomas Hooker 1586-1647. A leader of the founders in this commonwealth. A preacher of persuasive power. A statesman who based all civil authority on the free consent of the people."

marriages.officiant: Rev. Robert Chaloner, D.D., rector of Amersham (Susanna's aunt's husband)

sources.full: J.R. Hutchinson, 'The Rev. Thomas Hooker—An Alternative Descent,' NY G&B Record 48 (1917), p. 393
Josephine C. Frost, NY G&B Record 64 (Jan 1933), p. 2

narrative_blocks.header: Led 100 settlers from Cambridge to found Hartford
narrative_blocks.body: In the summer of 1636, Hooker led a company of one hundred men, women, and children from Newtown (Cambridge) to found Hartford — a hundred miles through wilderness with no path, guided only by a compass. They drove 160 cattle before them, cleared thickets with axes, built causeways from felled trees, rested in shady groves, and ate wild strawberries. They made the journey in a fortnight.

narrative_blocks.header: His 1638 sermon framed Connecticut's constitution
narrative_blocks.body: On May 31, 1638, preaching before the General Court assembled to frame Connecticut's constitution, Hooker declared: "The foundation of authority is laid in the free consent of the people." The Fundamental Orders of Connecticut, adopted January 14, 1639 — often called the world's first written democratic constitution — grew from this sermon.

narrative_blocks.header: Called the light of the western churches
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker was universally regarded by his contemporaries as the foremost preacher in New England — Cotton Mather called him "the light of the western churches."

narrative_blocks.header: He moderated the Hutchinson trial, then left
narrative_blocks.body: In 1637, Hooker served as one of two moderators over the three-week inquiry into Anne Hutchinson's antinomian doctrines — which culminated in her excommunication and banishment from Massachusetts Bay. Having completed his role, Hooker returned to Hartford before the trial concluded.

narrative_blocks.header: No portrait exists — only his descendants' faces
narrative_blocks.body: There is no known portrait of Thomas Hooker. The statue that stands before the Old State House in Hartford — near the site of the First Meeting House — was sculpted by comparing the likenesses of his descendants.

quotes.text: His genius would still haunt all the pulpits. He had great popularity and influence, especially with young ministers, to whom he was an oracle, and their principle library.

Having gone on, with much strength of voice and intention of spirit, about a quarter of an hour, he was at a stand and told the people, that God had deprived him of both his strength and matter, and so went forth and about half an hour after, returned again, and went on to very good purpose about tw
I giue vnto my sonne Samuell, in case the whole library come not to him... the summe of seuenty pounds.
Brother, I am going to receive mercy!
quotes.attribution: Thomas Hooker, deathbed, July 7, 1647 (Cotton Mather, Magnalia Christi Americana)

career.notes: Cared for Mrs. Joanna Drake; 'had his diet and lodging' in Drake household
Silenced by Bishop Laud for nonconformity
After silencing; John Eliot was assistant

education.notes: B.A. 1608; M.A. 1611; Fellow 1611-c.1618 — Entered divinity course; left before B.D.

narrative_blocks.header: They met in the same household in Surrey
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna Garbrand was working as a waiting-gentlewoman to Mrs. Joanna Drake at Esher, Surrey, when Thomas Hooker arrived in 1620 as curate of Esher church — "having his diet and lodging" in the Drake household. They married at Amersham on April 3, 1621, the ceremony performed by Susanna's own uncle by marriage, Rev. Robert Chaloner.

narrative_blocks.header: Granddaughter of Oxford's Reformation bookseller
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna descended from the Garbrands, a Calvinist family who fled the Low Countries for England. Her grandfather Garbrand Harks became the foremost Oxford bookseller of the Reformation and official stationer to the University, a post the family held for three generations.

narrative_blocks.header: Crossed on the Griffin in the great migration
narrative_blocks.body: In 1633 Susanna sailed to New England aboard the Griffin with her husband and children, part of the Puritan migration that brought some twenty thousand settlers before 1640. The family settled first at Cambridge, then helped found Hartford in 1636.

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried Elder William Goodwin after Hooker's death
narrative_blocks.body: After Thomas Hooker died in 1647, Susanna married Elder William Goodwin, a leading Hartford churchman. In 1654 a Hartford court punished Walter Gray for slandering the couple with a charge of adultery.

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived Hooker by nearly thirty years
narrative_blocks.body: Susanna outlived Thomas Hooker by almost thirty years, dying at Farmington in 1676 at about eighty-three. Through her eight children the Hooker line descended to figures including the financier J.P. Morgan and President William Howard Taft.

burialCemetery.name: Old Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Contains graves of 40 Revolutionary War soldiers. Massachusetts Historical Commission MACRIS MEN803.
